museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A sparse spring drawer: cartridge systems exist, but 1977's defining launches have not yet arrived.
museum.curatorNote: March keeps the record quiet rather than padding it.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a very early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: The archive has no confident March release shelf
items.body: Reviewed sources do not support a strong month-specific release list.

items.title: Cartridge consoles are still early adopters' objects
items.body: Studio II and Channel F exist, but the category is not yet the Atari-shaped mass memory.

items.title: Handheld electronic games are gathering momentum
items.body: Mattel Football belongs to 1977, but exact month-level dating should be treated cautiously.
items.sourceNote: Mattel Football sources place it in 1977.

items.title: Arcade machines remain local events
items.body: A new cabinet matters only when it arrives in your town, arcade or leisure centre.

items.title: UK gaming is still mostly public and imported
items.body: The visitor experience is coin-op, electronics shops and magazine glimpses rather than a dedicated games culture.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: No confident month-specific release shelf
items.body: The sources reviewed do not support a full list of videogame releases specifically for March 1977. This drawer stays sparse rather than turning year-level facts into fake launch dates.
items.sourceNote: Early console, arcade and computer-game records are often year-level, regional, mail-order, trade-show based, or retrospective.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, cartridges, handheld electronics and computing culture.

items.title: Atari Video Computer System
items.body: By March 1977, the VCS is the hardware object that makes cartridges feel like a home library rather than a technical curiosity.

items.title: RCA Studio II
items.body: Historically important because it is early; commercially vulnerable because its black-and-white keypad design is quickly outpaced.

items.title: Nintendo Color TV-Game
items.body: Nintendo's first home-console line is dedicated and Japan-only, but it begins a major hardware story.

items.title: Vector arcade hardware
items.body: Space Wars shows the arcade moving toward sharp line-drawn images that will become central to later classics.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: There is still no normal videogame magazine shelf. Electronics and computing magazines are the map.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online play was not a home visitor experience
items.body: For most players in March 1977, game discovery happened through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth. Networked play existed at institutional edges, not as a normal domestic habit.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later internet culture backward.

items.title: PLATO remains the hidden network
items.body: PLATO's games, messaging and shared terminals belong to the background of the era, but not to everyday UK consumer play.
items.sourceNote: PLATO context from early computer-game histories.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues and club newsletters are how players and hobbyists learn what exists.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the social feed
items.body: Scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet itself a public information system.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future still split between public machines and experimental home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: Arcades still feel richer than home, but home hardware is becoming more serious and more legible.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: The cartridge is the key change: a game can now be a separate object, a small plastic promise that the machine has a future.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: For a UK visitor, the story is delayed and uneven: historically important hardware appears before it becomes a normal local childhood memory.

items.title: The record is patchy because the medium is young
items.body: There are few clean launch days, few consumer reviews and no settled games press. The museum label often has to say: year-level, regional, uncertain.
